title: International Mobility Program: General Agreement on Trade in Services - Canada.ca
content:
## International Mobility Program: General Agreement on Trade in Services
This section contains policy, procedures and guidance used by IRCC staff. It is posted on the department’s website as a courtesy to stakeholders.
In these instructions, “officer” refers to employees of both Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) and the Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA).
The instructions on this page should be reviewed in conjunction with the following:
- Employer-specific work permits – General processing – International Mobility Program
- Assessing the genuineness of the offer of employment on a work permit application
- Conditions and validity period on work permits
- Public list of Employers who have been non-compliant
The temporary entry of business persons under the General Agreement on Trade in Services (GATS) can be facilitated without the need for a Labour Market Impact Assessment. Canada offers access for three categories of business persons: business visitors, intra-company transferees, and professionals.
Under GATS and in accordance with the IRPR, business visitors are work-permit exempt and qualify under R186(a), while intra-company transferees qualify under paragraph R205(a) with administrative codes C62 and C63. Professionals seeking entry under GATS may be granted work permits pursuant to R204(a) with administrative code T33, if they meet the criteria outlined below.
A GATS Professional is a person seeking to engage, as part of a services contract, in an activity at the professional level in a listed profession, where the contract is either:
- obtained by a foreign service provider (juridical person from another WTO Member) with a Canadian service consumer, or
- granted by a Canadian company to a foreign service provider that does not have a commercial presence in Canada
The professional must possess the necessary academic credentials and professional qualifications, which have been duly recognized, where appropriate, by the professional association in Canada. The Professionals category is designed to facilitate the short-term entry of a limited list of professionals delivering services under a contract in sectors where Canada has made commitments, excluding education, health-related, cultural, and sports services.
- Place of application
- Eligibility Restrictions and Non-Eligible Sectors
- Restrictions and Non-Eligible Sectors
- Documentary evidence
- Application assessment Review of the offer of employment
- Review of the offer of employment
- Final decision Approval Refusal

## Place of application
Applications for work permits under GATS may be made at a visa office or at a port of entry (POE).
Foreign nationals who are exempt from the requirement for a temporary resident visa, as well as foreign nationals who otherwise meet the requirements of R198, may apply for a work permit at the POE. For more information, consult: Temporary Foreign Worker Program (TFWP) and International Mobility Program (IMP): Persons who may apply at a port of entry.
Foreign nationals may apply for a work permit from within Canada if they meet the conditions set out in section 199 of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Regulations.
To be eligible for a work permit in the GATS Professionals category, a foreign national must:
- be a citizen of a Member nation of the World Trade Organization (WTO) or hold permanent resident status in Australia, New Zealand, Armenia or Switzerland; Note: WTO member nations (166 members as of August 2024) are listed on the World Trade Organization website .
- Note: WTO member nations (166 members as of August 2024) are listed on the World Trade Organization website .
- be seeking entry to work in an occupation listed in the GATS Professional Occupations & Qualification Requirements and meet the agreement’s criteria for that occupation;
- be qualified to work in that profession (degree or certification in a related educational program) as listed;
- have a valid, signed contract to provide a service between a Canadian service consumer (e.g., a Canadian business) and a foreign service provider from a WTO Member nation; and
- be compliant with all other requirements for temporary entry.
Under Canada's GATS commitments, professional occupations eligible for temporary entry into Canada are categorized into two groups:
- Group 1 Occupations and Group 2 Occupations
Each group is subject to specific conditions regarding how service contracts are obtained or granted, the commercial presence of the foreign service provider in Canada, and the eligibility of the Canadian service consumer. In addition to these contracting requirements, applicants must meet minimum educational qualifications, alternative credentials, and licensing obligations. Officers must assess applications based on these criteria to ensure compliance with GATS commitments.

### Restrictions and Non-Eligible Sectors
Even if an occupation is listed as eligible under GATS, the following sectors are not covered :
- Health-related services
- Recreational, cultural, and sports services
Contracts with personnel placement or personnel supply agencies do not qualify under GATS, even if the occupation is otherwise eligible.
A limit of 10 entrants per project applies to Senior Computer Specialists.
Geomatics Professionals must be working in aerial surveying or aerial photography.

## Documentary evidence
A foreign national must provide sufficient documentary evidence to satisfy an officer that they are eligible for entry.
The following documents are required:
- proof that they are a citizen of a WTO Member nation , or that they hold the right of permanent residence in Australia, New Zealand, Armenia or Switzerland
- evidence that they hold anoccupation identified in GATS Professional Occupations & Qualification Requirements
- copy of a signed contract between the foreign service provider and the Canadian service consumer: the contract may have been signed by a foreign service provider located in any Member nation (Group 1 Occupations) or granted by Canadian company engaged in substantive business in Canada, with the foreign service provided having no commercial presence in Canada (e.g., no office, branch, or subsidiary) (Group 2 Occupations)
- evidence that the foreign national has the professional qualifications as detailed in GATS Professional Occupations & Qualification Requirements (copies of degrees, diplomas, professional licences, accreditation or registration, etc.)
- where required, documentation from the professional association in Canada by the applicable provincial/territorial government indicating that the foreign national’s academic credentials and professional qualifications have been duly recognized
- evidence that they meet the eligibility criteria may include, but is not limited to, the following reference letters letter of support from the company job descriptions that outline the level of training acquired years of experience in the field degrees or certifications obtained in the field list of publications and awards (where applicable) a detailed description of the work to be performed in Canada
- letter of support from the company
- job descriptions that outline the level of training acquired
- years of experience in the field
- degrees or certifications obtained in the field
- list of publications and awards (where applicable)
- a detailed description of the work to be performed in Canada
- an offer of employment submitted through the Employer Portal or through alternate means if authorized the offer of employment provides confirmation of the pre-arranged employment; the proposed employer in Canada; the profession for which entry is sought; details of the position (title, duties, duration of employment, arrangements as to payment); and the educational qualifications or alternative credentials required for the position
- the offer of employment provides confirmation of the pre-arranged employment;
- the proposed employer in Canada;
- the profession for which entry is sought;
- details of the position (title, duties, duration of employment, arrangements as to payment); and
- the educational qualifications or alternative credentials required for the position

## Application assessment

### Review of the offer of employment
When assessing if the work permit application meets the requirements for a GATS Professional, officers should review the offer of employment that appears under the Employment Details tab in the Global Case Management System (GCMS) for information provided by the employer or the matching fields on the IMM 5802 form (if the employer was authorized to use it).
Exemption code (Administrative code)
Requirements Exemptions Met
Information in this field outlines how the job and the foreign national meet the regulatory and agreement requirements, including that the foreign national: holds an occupation that is included in the list of eligible professions under the GATS
- holds an occupation that is included in the list of eligible professions under the GATS
Ensure that the occupational code and title are in the appropriate TEER for the profession.
Maximum three months or 90 consecutive days within a twelve-month period. Note : extension of the employment authorization as a GATS Professional beyond 90 days is not permitted .
Note : extension of the employment authorization as a GATS Professional beyond 90 days is not permitted .
The foreign national must be seeking entry pursuant to a signed contract between the foreign service provider and a Canadian service consumer, and must work in one of the service sectors listed in GATS Professional Occupations & Qualification Requirements . Secondary employment is not permitted (prohibition on working for an employer who is not named on the offer of employment). The Professional’s foreign-based employer must be a legitimate and functioning business, established and actively engaged in providing goods and/or services. Employers that exist in name only, without evidence of real operations, would not qualify as “doing business.”
The foreign national must be seeking entry pursuant to a signed contract between the foreign service provider and a Canadian service consumer, and must work in one of the service sectors listed in GATS Professional Occupations & Qualification Requirements .
Secondary employment is not permitted (prohibition on working for an employer who is not named on the offer of employment).
The Professional’s foreign-based employer must be a legitimate and functioning business, established and actively engaged in providing goods and/or services. Employers that exist in name only, without evidence of real operations, would not qualify as “doing business.”
Duties and Job Requirements
These are the activities that the foreign national will be performing. Do they align with the profession? Are there specific requirements that align with the professional category?
These are the activities that the foreign national will be performing.
- Do they align with the profession?
- Are there specific requirements that align with the professional category?
There is no requirement that the foreign national be paid by the Canadian enterprise or in Canadian dollars.
There is no requirement that the foreign national be paid by the Canadian enterprise or in Canadian dollars.
Minimum Education Requirements
Do the educational requirements outlined in the offer of employment align with the minimum education requirements of the professional category? Are there additional or alternative requirements for this occupation defined in the NOC?
Do the educational requirements outlined in the offer of employment align with the minimum education requirements of the professional category?
Are there additional or alternative requirements for this occupation defined in the NOC?
Provincial/Federal Certification, Licensing or Registration
The employer should list any specific certification, licensing or registration required in Canada. Documented evidence should be provided with the application if the occupation is regulated by the province or territory; however, some occupations may require the foreign national to write an exam after they enter Canada: for example, for a licence from a regulated body or a first aid certificate.
The employer should list any specific certification, licensing or registration required in Canada.
Documented evidence should be provided with the application if the occupation is regulated by the province or territory; however, some occupations may require the foreign national to write an exam after they enter Canada: for example, for a licence from a regulated body or a first aid certificate.
Refer to Employer-specific work permits – General processing – International Mobility Program for further guidance.

#### Contract Requirements
Group 1 and Group 2 Occupations differ in how service contracts are obtained and granted.
- Group 1 Occupations: The contract must be obtained by a foreign service provider from a WTO Member nation . The foreign company may or may not have a commercial presence in Canada .
- Group 2 Occupations: The contract must be obtained by a foreign service provider from a WTO Member nation that does not have a commercial presence in Canada . The contract must be granted by a Canadian service consumer engaged in substantive business in Canada . Additional Conditions for Group 2 Occupations: The requirement that the Canadian service consumer must be engaged in substantive business in Canada is interpreted to mean that the enterprise is not a shell company or was not established solely to facilitate the entry of foreign workers. Officers will need to rely on information provided by the applicant, supported by documents from the employer in Canada, such as business registration, financial records, or evidence of ongoing operations. The requirement that the foreign service provider must not have a commercial presence in Canada can only be established based on information provided by the applicant. Officers should confirm that the professional is not seeking entry to provide services to their own company or employer, which has established itself in Canada merely to facilitate the entry of its own employees. If the foreign service provider has a Canadian-based subsidiary, branch, or entity, the contract does not qualify under GATS.
Group 2 Occupations: The contract must be obtained by a foreign service provider from a WTO Member nation that does not have a commercial presence in Canada . The contract must be granted by a Canadian service consumer engaged in substantive business in Canada .
Additional Conditions for Group 2 Occupations:
- The requirement that the Canadian service consumer must be engaged in substantive business in Canada is interpreted to mean that the enterprise is not a shell company or was not established solely to facilitate the entry of foreign workers. Officers will need to rely on information provided by the applicant, supported by documents from the employer in Canada, such as business registration, financial records, or evidence of ongoing operations.
- The requirement that the foreign service provider must not have a commercial presence in Canada can only be established based on information provided by the applicant. Officers should confirm that the professional is not seeking entry to provide services to their own company or employer, which has established itself in Canada merely to facilitate the entry of its own employees.
- If the foreign service provider has a Canadian-based subsidiary, branch, or entity, the contract does not qualify under GATS.

#### Credentials, Qualifications & Licensing
All applicants must possess the necessary academic and professional qualifications, which must be recognized by the relevant professional association in Canada, where required.
Academic equivalencies will be determined by the relevant equivalency services in Canada.

#### Eligible GATS Professional Occupations & Qualification Requirements
In processing applications from Professionals, it is essential that officers refer to the chart for eligible occupations in order to understand what credentials are required for each occupation and which provinces issue licences for the practice of those occupations.
Minimum educational requirement alternative credentials
Baccalaureate degree
Baccalaureate degree in agriculture or related science; plus 4 years of related experience
Licensing required in New Brunswick, Alberta & Quebec. Temporary licensing required in British Columbia
Baccalaureate degree in architecture
Provincial licence and certificate required to practise
Forestry Professionals
Baccalaureate degree in forestry management or forestry engineering, or a provincial licence
Licensing as a forester or forestry engineer is required in Alberta, British Columbia & Quebec
Geomatics Professionals
Baccalaureate degree in surveying, geography or environmental sciences; plus three (3) years related experience
Baccalaureate degree
Foreign Legal Consultants
Baccalaureate degree in law
Baccalaureate degree in urban planning
Senior Computer Specialists
Graduate degree in computer sciences or related discipline; and 10 years of experience in computer sciences.
Baccalaureate degree : A degree from an accredited academic institution in Canada or equivalent
Graduate degree : At least a Master’s degree from an accredited academic institution in Canada or equivalent
Provincial licence : Any document issued by a provincial/territorial government or under its authority which permits a person to engage in a regulated activity or profession
If a licence to practice in Canada is required, officers cannot issue a work permit unless the applicant has obtained, prior to arrival in Canada, a temporary or permanent licence from the appropriate province.
If the applicant presents a provincial licence, it is not necessary for officers to examine the documentation from a professional association or the applicant’s professional qualifications as the province has already done that, except in the case of Foreign Legal Consultants, Urban Planners and Senior Computer Specialists where the foreign-based employer cannot be established in Canada.
If no licence is required to practice in Canada, officers cannot issue a work permit unless the applicant can produce documentation from an appropriate professional association in Canada, indicating that their academic credentials and professional qualifications have been recognized.
If the applicant presents such documentation from the appropriate professional association in Canada, it is not necessary for officers to examine the applicant’s educational credentials as the professional association has already done that.
The work permit will be issued under the authority of paragraph R204(a) .
In GCMS, under the Application screen, officers should enter the information below in the specified fields.
Province of destination
The province of destination entered by the applicant should match the address of employment in the IMP offer of employment. This information is under the Employment Details – LMIA-exempt tab . If there is more than one location, officers should enter the primary location in the “Province of destination” field and the secondary location in the “User remarks” field.
The province of destination entered by the applicant should match the address of employment in the IMP offer of employment. This information is under the Employment Details – LMIA-exempt tab .
If there is more than one location, officers should enter the primary location in the “Province of destination” field and the secondary location in the “User remarks” field.
The city of destination entered by the applicant should match the address of employment in the IMP offer of employment. This information is under the Employment Details – LMIA-exempt tab. If there is more than one location, officers should enter “Unknown” in the “City of destination” field and “As per the offer” in the “User remarks” field.
The city of destination entered by the applicant should match the address of employment in the IMP offer of employment. This information is under the Employment Details – LMIA-exempt tab.
If there is more than one location, officers should enter “Unknown” in the “City of destination” field and “As per the offer” in the “User remarks” field.
Exemption code (Administrative code)
T33 This code is auto-populated from the IMP offer of employment.
This code is auto-populated from the IMP offer of employment.
The NOC code is auto-populated from the IMP offer of employment.
The NOC code is auto-populated from the IMP offer of employment.
Job title This is auto-populated from the IMP offer of employment.
This is auto-populated from the IMP offer of employment.
“A” number from the work permit application. This number is auto-populated from the work permit application, and it is what is used to “match” in the Portal. If the work permit application was submitted on paper, the officer must manually enter the number.
“A” number from the work permit application.
This number is auto-populated from the work permit application, and it is what is used to “match” in the Portal. If the work permit application was submitted on paper, the officer must manually enter the number.
Business operating name
Business operating name
Maximum 3 months or 90 consecutive days within a 12-month period. Note : extension of the employment authorization as a GATS Professional beyond the 90 days is not permitted .
Maximum 3 months or 90 consecutive days within a 12-month period.
Note : extension of the employment authorization as a GATS Professional beyond the 90 days is not permitted .
Note: If the employer is authorized to use an IMM 5802 form instead of completing the offer of employment through the Employer Portal, please see Employer-specific work permits – General processing – International Mobility Program .
If an officer is not satisfied that all the requirements of section R200 , including the assessment under paragraph R204(a) , are met, they must record their reasons and outline the rationale underlying the decision as well as the facts and elements considered. They must also provide an explanation for the decision in a case note.
A decision is reasonable and therefore defendable when another person is able to trace the decision maker’s reasoning, without encountering fatal flaws in the overarching logic, and is satisfied that there is a line of analysis within the given reasons that could reasonably lead the tribunal from the evidence before it to the decision maker’s conclusion.
The officer needs to engage with the documentary evidence that was provided by the applicant. Simply stating ‘I have reviewed the submissions and I am not satisfied that section R204 is met’ is not sufficient for another reasonable person to understand the logic of the decision without reviewing all of the evidence again.
Refusal reasons should clearly indicate which criteria or what requirement of section R200 was not met and explain how the conclusion was reached.
For assistance, officers can follow the steps in Decision making: Standard of review and process for making a reasonable decision .
Refusal grounds in the GCMS
The GCMS has standard text for refusal grounds. Officers should ensure that the refusal grounds selected for the refusal letter match the reasons that they have stated in their case note.
If an officer has reasonable grounds to believe that the applicant is not able to perform the work sought, they should select “paragraph R200(3)(a) You were not able to demonstrate that you will be able to adequately perform the work you seek .” Officers should ensure that they clearly indicate in their refusal notes why they are not satisfied.
Given that there is no specific refusal ground for GATS in GCMS, when an officer is not satisfied that the criteria have been met, they should select the refusal ground “Other” and add a short explanation in the Comments field that they are not satisfied that section R200 or paragraph R204(a) have been met.
